skill.name: reconnaissance-knowledge
skill.description: Guides authorized network reconnaissance, service enumeration, web discovery, and structured reporting for security assessments and labs.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized reconnaissance workflows.
skill.author: CharlesKozel

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptography, and hard link creation are false positives caused by markdown backticks, frontmatter text, and command syntax. The security risk is still high because the skill provides concrete active scanning, web brute forcing, vulnerability scan, default credential, and exploitation handoff guidance for arbitrary targets without enforcing authorization.

high_findings.title: Active Network Scanning Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill lists tools such as nmap, masscan, and netcat, then provides aggressive scan examples for discovering open ports and services on a placeholder target. This is dual-use guidance that can be applied to unauthorized systems if the user supplies a real target.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly names active network scanners and gives command examples for broad and aggressive scans. The context confirms real reconnaissance capability, even though the markdown itself is not executable.

high_findings.title: Web and Service Enumeration Against Targets
high_findings.description: The skill gives directory brute force, vulnerability scan, SMB, FTP, SSH, and database enumeration steps. It includes anonymous access checks, default credential testing, and user enumeration checks that can expose services or accounts.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions are specific operational reconnaissance steps, not abstract descriptions. They include network tools, target placeholders, and credential-related checks.

high_findings.title: Exploitation Handoff Recommendations
high_findings.description: The output template asks for potential attack vectors and recommended actions, and the final section tells the user to proceed to exploitation knowledge after reconnaissance. This goes beyond passive documentation and helps chain reconnaissance into exploitation planning.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly request exploitation approaches and a transition to an exploitation phase. The skill still states no exploitation during reconnaissance, so this is high risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Missing Authorization Guardrails
medium_findings.description: The purpose and best-practice sections describe gathering target information and adapting speed for playground or real environments, but they do not require proof of authorization or scope boundaries. This increases the chance of misuse against systems outside an approved assessment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill is clearly written for reconnaissance work and mentions playground and real environments. No authorization requirement is present in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Static Parser False Positives in Markdown
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick execution findings are caused by markdown inline code and fenced examples, not Ruby source execution. The weak cryptography and hard link findings also lack supporting evidence in the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is markdown and the cited content is prose or command text. I found no Ruby execution, weak cryptographic algorithm implementation, or hard link creation at those locations.

low_findings.title: Placeholder URLs and Private IP Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL and IP findings are placeholders such as http://TARGET and a private lab-style address in example output. They do not show data exfiltration endpoints, but they reinforce the network reconnaissance use case.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use a target placeholder, and the IP address appears only in example output. I found no fixed third-party endpoint or credential exfiltration logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Aggressive Reconnaissance Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends fast full-port scans, service detection, default scripts, and vulnerability scripts. These commands can generate intrusive network traffic when used outside a controlled scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and flags are directly visible and are intended for active scanning. Legitimate lab use is possible, but the pattern is still dangerous in marketplace context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Directory Brute Force and Vulnerability Scanning
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to run web directory brute forcing and vulnerability scanners against a target URL. This can identify hidden content and known vulnerabilities on live systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands are concrete and target-facing. The semantic context is web enumeration and vulnerability discovery.

dangerous_patterns.title: Default Credential and Account Enumeration Checks
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends anonymous FTP access checks, default database credential attempts, and SSH user enumeration checks. These steps can cross from inventory into account probing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines explicitly describe credential and user enumeration tests. They are common authorized assessment tasks but are risky without strict scope controls.

content.value_statement: Security teams need consistent reconnaissance notes before deeper testing. This skill organizes authorized scanning, service enumeration, web discovery, and reporting into repeatable phases.
actual_capabilities: Breaks reconnaissance into quick, deep, and alternative collection layers.
Lists common tools for port discovery, web enumeration, DNS lookup, and service checks.
Provides example workflows for TCP, UDP, HTTP, SMB, FTP, SSH, and database services.
Defines structured report fields for ports, services, technologies, paths, and likely vectors.
Suggests when to save raw output and update a state file.
limitations: It does not verify that a target is authorized for testing.
It does not run tools by itself; it only provides guidance and examples.
Some examples are intrusive and may be unsafe on production systems.
It does not include remediation guidance for discovered vulnerabilities.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Lab Recon Plan
use_cases.description: Create a phased checklist for a controlled practice host before starting manual analysis.

use_cases.title: Standardize Assessment Notes
use_cases.description: Convert scan results into consistent sections for ports, services, web paths, and next steps.

use_cases.title: Review Enumeration Coverage
use_cases.description: Check whether an authorized assessment covered common network, web, and service discovery areas.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Recon Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for an authorized target
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a beginner reconnaissance checklist for an authorized lab target. Include port discovery, service detection, web checks, and notes to collect.

prompt_templates.title: Organize Service Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me organize these authorized scan findings into services, versions, web technologies, interesting paths, and follow-up questions.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Coverage Gaps
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of an assessment workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this authorized reconnaissance workflow and identify missing enumeration areas, risky assumptions, and safer alternatives for production systems.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Handoff Summary
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced transition to later assessment phases
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a concise handoff summary for an authorized assessment. Include discovered services, likely risk areas, evidence to preserve, and scope reminders.

output_examples.input: I have an authorized lab host with SSH and HTTP open.
output: Start with service version confirmation for SSH and HTTP.
Enumerate web technologies, visible routes, and interesting response codes.
Record service versions, evidence files, and unanswered questions for review.

output_examples.input: Review my reconnaissance notes for coverage gaps.
output: TCP service coverage is present, but UDP discovery is missing.
HTTP discovery includes paths, but technology fingerprinting needs confirmation.
Add scope notes so later testing stays within the authorized target list.

output_examples.input: Summarize findings for a team handoff.
output: The target exposes remote access, web, and file sharing services.
The highest follow-up areas are outdated web software and anonymous share access.
Preserve raw scan output and separate confirmed findings from hypotheses.

best_practices: Use this skill only for systems where you have explicit written authorization.
Start with low-impact discovery before increasing scan intensity.
Keep raw evidence, scope notes, and assumptions separate in the final report.
anti_patterns: Do not use the workflows against public systems without approval.
Do not treat example findings as confirmed vulnerabilities.
Do not skip scope validation before credential or account checks.

faq.question: Does this skill execute scans automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a markdown knowledge base with workflows and command examples.

faq.question: Is this safe for production environments?
faq.answer: Use caution. Some examples are aggressive and should be adapted for approved production testing.

faq.question: Can it help with authorized penetration tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It can structure reconnaissance planning, evidence collection, and handoff notes.

faq.question: Does it include exploitation steps?
faq.answer: It says reconnaissance should avoid exploitation, but it also suggests exploitation handoff planning.

faq.question: What tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It mentions tools for port scanning, web enumeration, service checks, DNS lookup, and output parsing.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The skill gives concrete active scanning and enumeration guidance that can be misused without authorization.
